sentences.text: My name is Andrew Furst, F-U-R-S-T.
The same, only pronounced slightly differently.
It's spelled the same, but with two dots on top of the U.
My first name is Andrew.
But at birth, it was Andre.
Yes, on-- on false papers, I used Furthos.
F-U-R-T-H-O-S.
My birth date is the 9th of March, 1927, which makes me 69.
In a place called Nagykanizsa in the south-- southwest of Hungary.
Well, it was a sort of a city in its own right.
I-- I can't say-- I would-- I would imagine a place called Kaposvar, which was larger than Nagykanizsa.
Well, as in most towns of that size in Hungary, about 10% of the population would have been Jewish.
And their life was lived around the synagogue and the various Jewish organizations, such as young men's club and the women's clubs and that sort of thing-- the [NON-ENGLISH].
And as far as I can tell now, there was no contact between Jews and non-Jews at all.
When I say no contact, no social contact.
I-- I meant, obviously, my father was a merchant.
He had any number of non-Jewish customers.
But I don't ever remember non-Jews coming to visit us in our flat or vice versa.
In the street?
Well.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] anti-Semitism was there.
But nevertheless, we accepted it as-- as one of the facts of life.
And you just lived with it.
Well, my-- one of my earliest memories would be being beaten up by kids at the-- at the playground.
We lived in flats, so most kids spent most of their time at the-- in the park at the playground.
And often, you got a beating because you were a Jew.
Now, and that was before I even knew what a Jew was, before I'd been to-- to school.
I remember kids punching us saying, bloody Jews, you killed our God.
And later, when I learned to read, I remember anti-Semitic, anti-Jewish slogans from when end of town to the other, painted on walls, railings, whatever.
Hungarian.
My father had a shop in the Main Street, and in fact, it was in the building of the Jewish community.
And he was a merchant, and he made quite a good middle class living out of it.
All the Jews in the town were-- you could describe them as middle class.
I have a feeling we had three rooms.
We lived there for quite a while, but it wasn't the earliest flat that I remember.
And it was quite well appointed, with leather arm chairs and sort of, I would think, Italian furniture.
They were quite well-to-do.
They had a-- my parents had a cook, a young Croat girl.
And when I say young, she was 25, 30.
Because we were right beside the Croatian border, and there are-- were many Croats to this day.
And then, when I got a bit older, they got a German speaking governess because they felt [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Jewish middle class kids ought to be able to speak German.
Not really, no-- well, obvious-- she couldn't speak a word of Hungarian.
So I-- I simply had to learn German to be able to communicate with her.
Look, I'm-- I'm not altogether sure.
But my grandparents spoke Yiddish.
Whenever they wanted to say something that we kids weren't supposed to understand, they spoke to each other in Yiddish.
They never taught my parents either-- either Yiddish or German.
And as I look back now, I think probably German was supposed to replace Yiddish as a-- as a second language.
And in fact, there were few Jews in Hungary who didn't speak German.
It was expected that you would.
My mother read German because she was educated in Vienna.
My father's German was poor, but nevertheless, he spoke it all right.
Leah.
She was born in a-- in a small village, not very far from where I was born, in the same county.
And as far as I can tell, they never really lived there.
They happened to have a shop at that particular time because none of her brothers or sisters were born in that village.
Her father kept the shop, as most Jews in that part of Hungary did.
In every village, there was usually one shop, occasionally a shop and a pub, and both were-- belonged to Jews.
And her father was-- her father owned apparently some land.
And he found out that somebody was poaching animals on his land.
And whoever he caught, shot him dead.
It was brought to court, and the-- the killer was acquitted.
And after that, my grandmother was left with five children and no means of support.
There was no social services at all.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my mother was adopted to a sister of hers who was childless.
And eventually, my grandmother remarried a widower with also quite a number of kids.
Joseph.
Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father was born in the same county, in a-- again, in a small village.
Even today, it's in the back and beyond.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] his father was a-- a pub keeper.
Maybe there is a more polite expression, but this is what it was.
But when I say it was my grandfather, it's wrong.
My grandfather never did any work.
It was my grandmother who ran the pub.
And my grandfather was studying the holy books all day every day, except on the Shabbat when he come back to the services.
The services were held in his house.
My mother and father's generation is a curious one.
But religious as my grandparents were, they didn't teach their children much knowledge of Hebrew or Judaism at all.
I think at that time it was probably regarded as the new patriotism or something like that to-- not to emphasize your Jewishness.
There is probably a parallel in Australia that the disappearance of Yiddish, for instance.
And yet, our parents sent us to-- to Jewish schools.
And as always, I suppose, Judaism was in a flux.
It wasn't-- at the time, of course, I thought it was something set in concrete.
But I realize today, it wasn't.
One brother.
He's about nine years older than me.
Yes, I spoke to him on the phone last night.
It was his birthday.
He lives in New York.
Nine years.
Ah, there is a-- a great lake not very far from where we lived.
And as well as that, my grandparents only lived a few kilometers from that lake.
So most of the time, we went there, not only holiday times, but quite often on weekends.
It was a Jewish elementary school.
Well, obviously, I would have to put it in a-- in an Australian context.
It would be the equivalent of a Melbourne orthodox school today.
Even though they would describe themselves as Neolog, which meant, in the Hungarian context, reform.
And that meant that they had the rabbis who were dressed like ministers of religion, much the same as in Australia, for instance, Rabbi Danglow.
And the sermon was preached in Hungarian, which in those days, was quite-- quite progressive.
No, that was the only one I ever went to.
Not an awful lot, really.
We knew that we were different, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but there was really no persecution.
You may get a beating.
You might even [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] get remarks passed on you.
But it wasn't dangerous.
For instance, on the Day of Atonement, the Jewish kids were the butt of every joke in town, you know.
As you were walking to the synagogue or some place-- are you-- are you starving?
You know, this sort of thing.
But that's probably the worst.
I do remember though Easter processions.
And that was a time when it really wasn't advisable for Jewish kids to be around.
It was a big thing, you know.
There were two churches in town.
And the procession went from one to the other.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it wasn't really tactful for a Jew to be around, put it that way.
They moved to Budapest.
And that was really the lifesaver.
If they would have stayed in Nagykanisza, none of us would be here today.
Basically business reasons.
My father thought he'd do better in-- in Budapest.
Not a lot, but nevertheless, you couldn't shut yourself away from it, even a kid who was-- who knew nothing about politics.
You know, a six, seven, eight-year-old kid knows nothing.
But nevertheless, the Berlin Olympic Games, for instance, brought it home.
And then later, the Spain-- the Spanish Civil War would have been a catalyst to political consciousness as the Vietnam War would have been to a lot of Australian kids.
Oh, yes, yes.
Well, I don't remember them obviously, just the flavor of it.
Naturally, Jews couldn't be in-- in favor of Hitler and company.
Never, no.
Look, there-- there were Zionists around.
And the Jewish kids who were in Zionist organizations-- and obviously, there were Zionist organizations-- it was discouraged by our parents, our teachers, our rabbis.
It was in bad [? order. ?]
I was really quite ignorant about it.
At that time, as a little kid, I was really quite ignorant.
I didn't know what it was about.
I mean, obviously, we were citizens of Hungary.
And of-- to be sure, of Jewish persuasion, but we were brought up to be patriotic Hungarians.
And it simply didn't make sense to pick yourself up and go to-- to the Middle East.
That was a private school.
And put it in the Melbourne context, well, let's say Melbourne grammar-- Scotch, if you like.
And-- but that was 1936, 1937.
And by then, anti-Semitism was-- was really-- there, it was quite unbearable.
Well, the teachers unfailingly picked on you.
And there were some who were sympathetic.
The headmaster, for instance, was a reasonable man.
But one or two teachers were absolutely rabid anti-Semites.
And the children in my class were not too bad, because they put all the Jews in the one class together with all the Protestants.
So that there were four or five parallel classes that were 100% Catholic.
And then there was one class half Jewish, half Protestant, and a sprinkling of Catholics to make up the numbers.
So-- and Protestants and Jews in Hungary tended to feel reasonably happy about each other.
You felt more at home in Protestant company than in Catholic company.
And so there was no-- no great problems with-- with classmates.
I would imagine about 15.
Yes, yes.
I remember one boy in particular who always stuck up for-- for Jewish classmates.
And incidentally, he was the top scholar.
So he could probably afford to.
Yes, he was a tall boy.
But he had a kind of an authority about him.
He was a help.
Well, obviously, It's the outbreak of war.
I can't really describe it for the simple reason because it meant absolutely nothing for us.
You got to remember that we lived in-- in a neutral country.
And it really meant nothing.
No.
No.
And of course, most people didn't believe that it would last, in Hungary or elsewhere.
It wasn't of-- of any great consequence at the time.
We lived in the 5th district, which was one of the Jewish districts of Budapest.
Lipotvaros, at that time, and then they changed it to Szentistvan, St.
Stephen Town.
It was [NON-ENGLISH] 32.
Yes.
That was a smallish apartment with just two bedrooms in a modern house.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I would imagine a large majority of the tenants would have been Jewish.
It was five stories high, one block away from the Danube.
And the-- on each floor, there would have been four or five flats, I think.
Well, my mother, my father and myself.
My brother, at that time, was already in the army.
Yes, well, at 18, all boys had to go into the army.
And since he matriculated, they sent him to officer school.
And then in the course of time, all Jews were chucked out of officer school, taken out of uniform, given a yellow armband, and eventually, sent off to Russia on forced labor.
There is nothing really to describe.
It was quite-- well, sort of an everyday existence.
I went to school.
I studied.
Played chess with my mates.
Occasionally, go down to the park, ice skating in the-- in the winter, swimming in the summer-- quite a reasonable existence.
Two years.
I was begging my parents to send me somewhere else.
The anti-Semitism was really just too much.
And so finally, they sent me to a state school.
I honestly. don't remember if it had a name.
It must have, but I can't remember it.
No, no, it was a state school.
Yes, there were a few.
Much less, yes, it was, in fact, nonexistent.
The teachers were reasonable.
Our classmates were reasonable.
I had no problems.
A different world, it was.
Two years.
I don't exactly know why.
But I went to a-- then I went again to a Jewish school, the only Jewish high school in Budapest.
It must have been 1940, I think, 1930-- yes, 1940 it would have been.
Not a hell of a lot.
I remember the synagogue.
It was the synagogue that my mother used to go to, and I knew the rabbi very well, a very decent bloke, a youngish bloke.
Most rabbis were old with a-- a long beard.
He was clean shaven.
And at state school, he was also a-- a religious teacher.
So I knew him quite well.
Yes, his name was Joseph Berkovitz.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I believe he was strongly anti-Nazi, and put the idea in our heads that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we got to watch it.
Watch our step.
And after the communist takeover, he became strongly anti-communist.
And he died of a heart attack as a young man.
Well, of course, every change, every political change brought its changes in its wake.
Yes, of course, things changed.
Well, the same as the Nazis, it wasn't-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there wasn't a cutoff point.
Obviously, the communists came in to great influence with-- as the Russians came, as the Red Army occupied the country.
But it took them several years to take over unlimited power.
Things were becoming worse all the time all along.
And they were tightening up the situation all the time, you know.
But nevertheless, it was livable until the Germans arrived in 1944.
It was-- it was quite a reasonable life.
But I got to add the footnote there that people who were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not born in Hungary or were not Hungarian citizens, they had an awful life even then.
They were taken to the border.
They were literally chucked out of the country.
And on the other side of the border, as likely or not, the Germans murdered them.
Well, look, anyone who-- whose grandparents were not born in Hungary, you had to be able to prove your-- your residency in Hungary to 1850.
And so anyone who couldn't prove Hungarian citizenship was in big trouble.

But the Jews who were Hungarian citizens, that lulled them into a false security saying that whatever happened to Jews in-- in Austria or in Poland, that can't happen to us because we fought for this country and so on and so forth.
Our ancestors go back hundreds of years, so we are safe.
Yes, some of them were, yes.
